<!--
More details on the background on the case study topic as well its relation to biomedical science.
Videos or images may be helpful so this includes an example of embedding a video.
-->

# **Context**
***

Outline: 

* microbiomes are important because the microbes that live in and around us affect human and environmental health
* specifically, the gut microbiome has been linked to many diseases
* the infant gut microbiome has been linked to diseases later in life
* an important research question is how a mother's gut microbiome affects the gut microbiome of an infant
* metagenomic sequencing gives a comprehensive look into the gut microbiome but is expensive. Therefore it is useful to compare samples across studies in a meta-analysis to increase power and draw conclusions across datasets.
* meta-analyses require care, because there may be systematic differences across original studies that need to be accounted for
* in a microbiome sample, sequencing depth is the number of reads (times we observe a microbe) in each sample. This does not necessarily measure a biological quantity, but instead a technical quantity, and may differ across sampling procedures and sequencing procedures
* therefore, in this meta-analysis, we want to investigate the association of sequencing depth and original study, while also considering whether samples came from a mother or infant 
* we want to know how comparable these studies are in terms of sequencing depth 
  
***
